workout.description: Chest Day workout focusing on bodybuild training.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.
instructions: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.

Lie supine on the bench with your head, upper back and glutes in contact with the bench, feet flat on the floor and a small natural arch in the lower back; retract and depress your shoulder blades to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists should be stacked over the elbows and forearms vertical when the bar is over the chest.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar out over the chest while keeping the scapulae stable; lock the elbows briefly before starting the set.
Inhale and lower the bar in a controlled manner to the mid-chest/sternum (around nipple level), keeping the elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso to protect the shoulders.
Allow the bar to touch or come very close to the chest without bouncing; maintain tension in the pectorals and keep the feet and glutes braced.
Exhale and press the bar upward explosively while keeping the bar path slightly arced toward the hips until the arms are fully extended but not hyperextended.
Pause briefly at the top with the shoulders stable, then begin the next rep. Maintain consistent form and breathing throughout the set.
Use a spotter or safety pins in the rack for heavy sets, and avoid flaring the elbows excessively or letting the lower back lift off the bench.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a compound pressing movement performed lying supine on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and anterior delts. It involves lowering a loaded barbell to the chest and pressing it back up to full arm extension with controlled technique.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and place a pair of dumbbells nearby.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and place a pair of dumbbells nearby.
Sit on the bench with the dumbbells resting on your thighs, feet flat on the floor for stability.
Use your legs to help lift the dumbbells as you lie back so the weights are beside your upper chest; hold them with a neutral or slightly supinated grip.
Retract your shoulder blades and press your upper back into the bench so your chest is proud (maintain a stable supine position on the incline).
Plant your feet and brace your core to avoid excessive arching of the spine.
Press the dumbbells upward and slightly together in a controlled path until your arms are almost fully extended (do not lock the elbows).
Exhale as you press up, pause briefly at the top with control, then inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the start position at the upper chest level.
Keep the elbows at a moderate angle (not flared excessively) and the wrists neutral throughout the movement.
Repeat for desired repetitions and use a spotter for heavy sets or if you cannot safely return the weights to your thighs/stand them up.
exercises.description: The dumbbell incline bench press is performed lying supine on an incline bench and pressing dumbbells upward to emphasize the upper portion of the chest. It builds strength and mass in the upper pectorals while also recruiting the front delts and triceps.

exercises.name: Cable Bent Over Single Arm Crossover
exercises.notes: Set a single handle on a cable pulley at about shoulder to chest height and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Set a single handle on a cable pulley at about shoulder to chest height and select an appropriate weight.

Stand to the side of the pulley with a staggered or shoulder-width stance and hinge forward at the hips until your torso is roughly 30–45° from vertical (slight bend in knees); maintain a neutral spine and brace your abs.
Grab the handle with the hand farthest from the machine, arm extended out to the side and palm facing down or slightly inward; allow a soft bend in the elbow.

Keep your shoulder down and stable, then exhale and pull the handle in a horizontal arc across your body toward the center of your chest, leading with the elbow and squeezing the pectorals at the end range.
Pause briefly with the chest contracted, keeping the torso still (avoid rotating the ribs or shoulders), then inhale and slowly reverse the motion to return the hand to the start under control.
Perform the desired reps, then switch sides and repeat. Maintain wrist neutrality and controlled tempo throughout.
exercises.description: A single-arm cable movement performed from a bent-over stance that sweeps the working arm across the body to load and compress the chest fibers, emphasizing the inner and upper pectoral region while the torso is stabilized.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Fly
exercises.notes: Step 1: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet planted on the floor and a neutral spine; hold a dumbbell in each hand above your chest with palms facing each other and elbows slightly bent.
instructions: Step 1: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet planted on the floor and a neutral spine; hold a dumbbell in each hand above your chest with palms facing each other and elbows slightly bent.
Step 2: Brace your core and gently retract and depress your shoulder blades to stabilize the shoulder girdle throughout the movement.

Step 3: Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc, keeping the same slight bend in your elbows and stopping when your upper arms are about parallel to the floor or you feel a comfortable stretch in the chest (avoid dropping below bench level).

Step 4: Pause briefly at the bottom without relaxing the shoulders, then exhale and drive the dumbbells back up in the same arc by contracting the pectorals until the weights meet or are close together over your sternum.
Step 5: Maintain controlled tempo (no bouncing), keep elbows fixed at the same angle, and avoid locking the arms at the top; repeat for desired reps.
exercises.description: Lying flat on a bench while holding dumbbells, the dumbbell fly opens the arms in a wide arc and brings them together to isolate and stretch the chest (pectorals). Movement emphasizes a controlled, arcing motion with a slight bend in the elbows to protect the shoulder joint.

exercises.name: Cable Incline Fly
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench between two cable towers (about 30–45° incline).
instructions: Set an incline bench between two cable towers (about 30–45° incline). Attach single D‑handles and set the pulleys to low or just below chest height so the cables travel upward across your chest.
Sit on the bench and lie back so your head rests on the pad; grip one handle in each hand with a neutral or palms‑facing posture and feet flat on the floor.
Retract your shoulder blades and brace your core so your back stays pressed into the bench (inclined supine position). Keep a slight, fixed bend in your elbows throughout the exercise.
Start with your arms out to the sides in line with your chest (open position) and inhale, then exhale as you bring the handles together in a wide arc toward the centerline of your upper chest.
Pause briefly at the peak contraction, focusing on squeezing the pectorals and keeping the elbows slightly bent and stable (avoid driving motion from the wrists or excessive shoulder elevation).
Slowly reverse the movement on the inhale, controlling the cables as you return to the start position until you feel a stretch in the chest without losing scapular retraction.
Repeat for the desired reps, maintaining core engagement and avoiding excessive momentum or arching of the lower back.
exercises.description: Lying on an incline bench placed between two cable towers, you perform a bilateral fly motion with a slight elbow bend to concentrically bring the handles together and target the upper chest. The movement emphasizes a horizontal adduction arc while the core stabilizes the torso.

exercises.name: Lever Seated Fly
exercises.notes: Select an appropriate weight and grasp the handles with a neutral or palms-facing grip, arms out to the sides with a slight bend in the elbows (no locked joints).
instructions: Sit on the machine with your back firmly against the pad and feet flat on the floor; adjust the seat so the handles are roughly at mid-chest level.
Select an appropriate weight and grasp the handles with a neutral or palms-facing grip, arms out to the sides with a slight bend in the elbows (no locked joints).
Brace your core and keep your chest up and shoulders down and back throughout the movement (seated, back supported).
Exhale and pull the handles together in a smooth arc in front of your chest, leading with the elbows and focusing on squeezing the pectorals at the end range.
Pause briefly at full contraction while maintaining the slight elbow bend and scapula position (do not let shoulders shrug).
Inhale and slowly return the handles to the start position under control, allowing a stretch in the chest but avoiding excessive shoulder extension.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining consistent tempo and full contact with the back pad.
Common form cues: keep elbows slightly bent, avoid using momentum or leaning forward, and do not let wrists or forearms drive the movement—lead with the chest.
exercises.description: Seated lever fly performed on a chest fly machine to isolate and contract the pectorals by bringing the arms together in front of the torso. The movement uses a slight elbow bend and a controlled arc to emphasize chest contraction.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
Grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, wrists stacked over elbows and thumbs wrapped around the bar.
Retract and depress the shoulder blades (pinch the shoulder blades together) to create a stable chest platform.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar over the chest so it is balanced and steady.
Inhale, lower the bar under control to the mid-chest (sternum) keeping elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso.
Pause briefly with the bar touching or just above the chest while maintaining tightness through the core and legs.
Exhale and drive the bar upward by extending the elbows, pressing the bar back to the start position while keeping wrists neutral.
Avoid excessive elbow flare or bouncing the bar off the chest; maintain a controlled tempo and keep feet planted.
After the set, carefully re-rack the bar by guiding it back onto the bench supports before releasing your grip.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a supine pressing exercise performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also working the triceps and anterior delts. The lifter lowers a barbell to the chest and presses it back to full arm extension under control.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and place a pair of dumbbells nearby.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and place a pair of dumbbells nearby.
Sit on the bench with the dumbbells resting on your thighs, feet flat on the floor for stability.
Use your legs to help lift the dumbbells as you lie back so the weights are beside your upper chest; hold them with a neutral or slightly supinated grip.
Retract your shoulder blades and press your upper back into the bench so your chest is proud (maintain a stable supine position on the incline).
Plant your feet and brace your core to avoid excessive arching of the spine.
Press the dumbbells upward and slightly together in a controlled path until your arms are almost fully extended (do not lock the elbows).
Exhale as you press up, pause briefly at the top with control, then inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the start position at the upper chest level.
Keep the elbows at a moderate angle (not flared excessively) and the wrists neutral throughout the movement.
Repeat for desired repetitions and use a spotter for heavy sets or if you cannot safely return the weights to your thighs/stand them up.
exercises.description: The dumbbell incline bench press is performed lying supine on an incline bench and pressing dumbbells upward to emphasize the upper portion of the chest. It builds strength and mass in the upper pectorals while also recruiting the front delts and triceps.

exercises.name: Cable Low Fly
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single-handle grips; select a moderate weight.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single-handle grips; select a moderate weight.
Stand centered between the towers in a staggered or shoulder-width stance with knees soft and core braced (slight forward lean at the hips).
Reach down and grab each handle with a neutral or slightly supinated grip, palms facing in or up, elbows slightly bent and fixed in that position throughout the movement.
Begin with the arms out to the sides and low (handles near hips or knees) and inhale.
Exhale and pull the handles upward and inward in a wide arc, keeping the elbows slightly bent, bringing the hands together or in front of the chest/upper sternum.
Pause briefly at the peak contraction and actively squeeze the pectorals while avoiding excessive shoulder shrugging.
Slowly reverse the motion under control, allowing the cables to return the arms to the start position while maintaining tension in the chest.
Repeat for desired repetitions and re-rack safely; keep torso stable and avoid using momentum.
exercises.description: Standing cable low fly is a low-to-high cable chest fly that targets the pectorals by sweeping the arms upward and together from low pulley positions; performed standing with a slight forward lean and a soft bend in the elbows to emphasize the chest contraction.

exercises.name: Lever Standing Pec Fly
exercises.notes: Set the lever machine handles so they are roughly at mid-chest/shoulder height and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Set the lever machine handles so they are roughly at mid-chest/shoulder height and select an appropriate weight.
Stand centered between the lever arms with feet about shoulder-width apart and a soft bend in the knees; maintain a neutral spine and a slight forward lean from the hips.
Grasp the handles with a neutral or palms-in grip and raise your arms out to the sides so they are roughly parallel to the floor; maintain a slight bend in the elbows throughout (start position).
Exhale and bring the handles together in front of your chest by squeezing the pectorals, keeping the elbow angle constant and avoiding shoulder shrugging or excessive forward elbow travel.
Pause briefly at peak contraction with hands meeting or close in front of the chest, focusing on contracting the pectorals.
Inhale and slowly control the levers back to the start position until you feel a stretch across the chest; do not let the weights slam back.
Repeat for the prescribed reps. Keep shoulders down and back, core braced, and avoid rotating the torso or using momentum.
exercises.description: A standing machine lever pec fly that isolates the chest by bringing two arms together in front of the body from a wide, horizontal start. Performed standing with a slight forward lean to maximize pectoral contraction while the delts and serratus anterior assist and stabilize.

exercises.name: Cable Middle Fly
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to about mid-chest height and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to about mid-chest height and select an appropriate weight.
Stand centered between the towers in a staggered stance (one foot slightly forward) with a slight bend in the knees and a neutral spine.
Grasp the handles with a neutral or palms-facing grip and extend your arms out to the sides so there is tension on the cables; keep a small, constant bend in the elbows.
Retract your shoulder blades and keep the shoulders down; brace your core to prevent torso movement.
Exhale and bring the handles together in front of your chest in a wide arc, leading with the elbows and squeezing the pectorals at the end range.
Inhale and slowly reverse the motion with control, allowing the arms to return to the starting position until you feel a gentle stretch in the chest.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, avoiding torso leaning, shoulder shrugging, or locking the elbows.
exercises.description: Standing bilateral cable fly performed with the pulleys set at mid-chest height; the exercise horizontally adducts the arms in a controlled arc to load and contract the pectorals. A staggered stance and slight elbow bend are used to stabilize and protect the joints while the chest performs the work.

exercises.name: Cable Standing Single Arm Fly
exercises.notes: Set a single handle on a cable pulley at about shoulder to mid-chest height and select a moderate weight.
instructions: Set a single handle on a cable pulley at about shoulder to mid-chest height and select a moderate weight.
Stand side-on to the machine with feet staggered (one foot slightly forward) for a stable base and brace your core (standing position).
Reach across and grab the handle with the working hand, palm facing in or slightly up; allow the arm to start out to the side with a small, natural bend in the elbow (not locked).
Retract the shoulder blade slightly and maintain a tall posture with shoulders down; keep the non-working arm relaxed or placed on your hip for balance.
Exhale and bring the hand across your body in a wide arc, driving horizontal adduction from the shoulder so the hand finishes in front of the chest or slightly across midline.
Pause briefly at the peak contraction while keeping the elbow slightly bent and the torso stable (avoid rotating or leaning excessively).
Inhale and slowly resist the cable as you return the arm back to the starting position with control, feeling the stretch in the pectoral at the end range.
Repeat for desired reps, then switch sides. Maintain core bracing and a fixed slight elbow bend throughout to protect the joint and target the pectorals.
exercises.description: A single-arm cable fly performs a horizontal adduction movement of the shoulder to isolate the chest (pectorals) unilaterally while the core stabilizes the torso. The exercise emphasizes controlled inward movement of the arm across the body with a slight elbow bend.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Fly
exercises.notes: Step 1: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet planted on the floor and a neutral spine; hold a dumbbell in each hand above your chest with palms facing each other and elbows slightly bent.
instructions: Step 1: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet planted on the floor and a neutral spine; hold a dumbbell in each hand above your chest with palms facing each other and elbows slightly bent.
Step 2: Brace your core and gently retract and depress your shoulder blades to stabilize the shoulder girdle throughout the movement.

Step 3: Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc, keeping the same slight bend in your elbows and stopping when your upper arms are about parallel to the floor or you feel a comfortable stretch in the chest (avoid dropping below bench level).

Step 4: Pause briefly at the bottom without relaxing the shoulders, then exhale and drive the dumbbells back up in the same arc by contracting the pectorals until the weights meet or are close together over your sternum.
Step 5: Maintain controlled tempo (no bouncing), keep elbows fixed at the same angle, and avoid locking the arms at the top; repeat for desired reps.
exercises.description: Lying flat on a bench while holding dumbbells, the dumbbell fly opens the arms in a wide arc and brings them together to isolate and stretch the chest (pectorals). Movement emphasizes a controlled, arcing motion with a slight bend in the elbows to protect the shoulder joint.

exercises.name: Cable Single Arm Low Fly
exercises.notes: Step 1: Set a single handle on a low cable pulley (near floor level).
instructions: Step 1: Set a single handle on a low cable pulley (near floor level). Stand with the cable side to your non-working side so the line of pull is across your body.
Step 2: Take a staggered or shoulder-width stance with a slight forward lean at the hips; brace your core and keep your torso tall and stable throughout the movement.
Step 3: Grasp the handle with a neutral or slightly supinated grip and raise your arm so the elbow is slightly bent (soft elbow) and aligned roughly with the lower chest at the start.

Step 4: Exhale and pull the handle upward and across your body in a wide arc, leading with the elbow and squeezing the pectoral at the end range where your hand finishes near the midline of the chest.
Step 5: Avoid excessive torso rotation or shrugging; keep the shoulder down and allow the scapula to protract slightly as you reach across to maximize chest contraction.
Step 6: Inhale and slowly return the handle along the same controlled path to the starting position, maintaining tension on the cable and control of the shoulder.
Step 7: Perform the desired reps, then switch sides; use a weight that allows full control and a smooth arc without jerking.
exercises.description: A unilateral cable fly performed with the pulley set low; the handle is pulled upward and across the body to horizontally adduct and internally rotate the shoulder, isolating the chest. This variation emphasizes the lower and inner portion of the pectoral while demanding scapular stability.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to parallel to the floor; keep the spine neutral (no rounding) and engage the abs and glutes.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and the barbell over mid-foot; use an overhand (pronated) grip slightly wider than shoulder width.
Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to parallel to the floor; keep the spine neutral (no rounding) and engage the abs and glutes.
Hold the bar with arms extended and shoulders protracted, chest up and eyes looking a few feet in front of you to maintain a neutral neck.
Initiate the rep by driving the elbows back and up, pulling the bar toward your lower chest / upper abdomen while squeezing the shoulder blades together.
Keep the elbows close to the body (not flaring excessively) and avoid using momentum from the hips; breathe out as you pull.
Lower the bar slowly and under control to the starting position with arms extended while maintaining the hip hinge and neutral spine; breathe in on the descent.
Repeat for the desired reps, controlling tempo and keeping the torso stable — if you cannot maintain a flat back, reduce weight or stop and reset.
exercises.description: Standing hip-hinged barbell row where you pull a loaded barbell toward the lower chest/upper abdomen; primarily targets the lats and upper back while requiring posterior chain and core stabilization. Maintain a flat back and drive the elbows back to retract the shoulder blades.

exercises.name: Cable seated row
exercises.notes: Initiate the movement by retracting your shoulder blades, then drive your elbows straight back keeping them close to your body while pulling the handle toward your lower sternum/upper abdomen.
instructions: Sit on the bench facing the cable machine with feet braced on the footplate and knees slightly bent (seated, upright position).
Grab the handle (V-bar or close grip) with a neutral or overhand grip and extend your arms so the weight stack is lifted and shoulders are protracted; maintain a neutral spine and chest slightly up.
Initiate the movement by retracting your shoulder blades, then drive your elbows straight back keeping them close to your body while pulling the handle toward your lower sternum/upper abdomen.
Keep the torso stable—avoid excessive leaning or using momentum; maintain a slight forward trunk angle only if required to reach the handle, then return to a tall, braced posture as you pull.

Squeeze the shoulder blades and lats at the end range for a brief 1–2 second pause, then slowly extend the arms forward under control to the start position while allowing the scapulae to move forward.
Breathe out while pulling and inhale while returning to the start; choose a weight that allows controlled form and full elbow extension between reps.
exercises.description: Seated cable row is a horizontal pulling exercise performed sitting with feet braced that primarily targets the lats while working the middle/upper back and arm pulling muscles. It emphasizes scapular retraction and elbow drive to pull the handle to the torso.

exercises.name: Lever Reverse Grip High Row (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Set the machine to a comfortable weight and adjust the seat and chest pad so your chest is firmly supported and your arms reach the handles with a slight bend at the elbows when extended.
instructions: Set the machine to a comfortable weight and adjust the seat and chest pad so your chest is firmly supported and your arms reach the handles with a slight bend at the elbows when extended.

Sit with your feet flat on the foot platform, chest pressed into the pad (chest-supported), core braced and eyes forward; use an underhand (reverse) grip on the handles with hands shoulder-width apart.
Start with your arms fully extended and shoulders relaxed — maintain a neutral spine and avoid leaning back or using momentum.
Initiate the movement by squeezing your shoulder blades together and pulling the handles in a smooth arc toward your upper chest/collarbone area.
Pause 1 second at the peak contraction, keeping the elbows close to the body and feeling the upper back and lats engage.
Slowly extend your arms to the start position under control, allowing the shoulder blades to protract slightly but maintaining tension in the back.
Repeat for the desired reps while keeping steady tempo; avoid jerking the weight or excessive torso movement.
exercises.description: Seated, chest-supported high row using a reverse (underhand) grip on a plate-loaded lever machine that emphasizes scapular retraction and the upper back while also recruiting the lats and biceps. Use a controlled pull to bring the handles toward the upper chest, pause and lower under control.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

Lower the bar slowly and under control to the starting position with arms extended while maintaining the hip hinge and neutral spine; breathe in on the descent.
Repeat for the desired reps, controlling tempo and keeping the torso stable — if you cannot maintain a flat back, reduce weight or stop and reset.
exercises.description: Standing hip-hinged barbell row where you pull a loaded barbell toward the lower chest/upper abdomen; primarily targets the lats and upper back while requiring posterior chain and core stabilization. Maintain a flat back and drive the elbows back to retract the shoulder blades.

exercises.name: Cable Lateral Pulldown with V-bar
exercises.notes: Attach a V-bar to the high pulley and reach up to grasp it with a neutral grip (palms facing each other).
instructions: Sit on the lat pulldown bench with knees secured under the pads and feet flat on the floor.
Attach a V-bar to the high pulley and reach up to grasp it with a neutral grip (palms facing each other).
Start with arms fully extended overhead, chest tall and a very slight backward lean at the hips (not rounded or excessively leaning back).
Exhale and pull the V-bar down by driving your elbows straight down toward your sides, leading with the elbows rather than the hands.
Bring the bar to the upper chest/sternum area while squeezing the lats and retracting the shoulder blades; keep the wrists neutral.
Pause for 1 second at the bottom while maintaining tension in the lats and upper back; avoid shrugging the shoulders toward the ears.
Inhale and slowly extend the arms back to the start position with a controlled tempo, fully stretching the lats without losing torso position.
Repeat for the desired reps; avoid using momentum or excessive torso lean—focus on a controlled, full-range vertical pulling motion.
exercises.description: A seated close-grip pulldown using a V-bar attachment that emphasizes the lats with a neutral (palms-facing) grip, while the arms and upper-back assist to pull the bar down toward the chest.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Palm Rotational Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Keep a neutral spine and neck (eyes toward the floor a few feet in front of you).
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart holding a dumbbell in each hand. Slightly bend the knees and hinge forward at the hips until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (bent-over position).
Keep a neutral spine and neck (eyes toward the floor a few feet in front of you). Let the dumbbells hang at arm's length beneath your shoulders with a pronated or neutral grip.
Brace your core and initiate the movement by pulling your shoulder blades back and down (scapular retraction).

Pull the dumbbells upward toward your lower ribs/waist by driving the elbows up and back. As you ascend, rotate your wrists so the palms turn (for example from pronation/neutral toward supination) at the top of the row.
Squeeze the upper back and rear delts at the top for a brief pause, keeping elbows close to the body and avoiding excessive torso rotation or momentum.
Lower the dumbbells slowly back to the starting position while rotating the palms back to the initial orientation, maintaining control throughout.
Repeat for the desired reps. Maintain the hip hinge, slight knee bend, and neutral spine for the entire set.
exercises.description: A bent-over dumbbell row variation where the wrists/palms rotate during the pull to increase scapular retraction and arm involvement. Performed in a hip-hinged position, it emphasizes the upper back while also engaging the lats, rear delts and arms.

exercises.name: Cable Straight Arm Pulldown
exercises.notes: Set a high pulley with a straight bar or rope attachment and select a light-to-moderate weight that allows strict form.
instructions: Set a high pulley with a straight bar or rope attachment and select a light-to-moderate weight that allows strict form.

Stand facing the machine about 1–2 feet away, feet shoulder-width apart and knees slightly bent; hinge forward at the hips so your torso is angled forward (standing bent-over position) with a neutral spine.
Reach up and grasp the bar with an overhand grip (palms facing down) about shoulder-width apart; maintain a small, natural bend in the elbows throughout (do not lock).
Brace your core and depress your shoulder blades slightly (avoid shrugging) so tension is felt in the lats before starting the pull.

Exhale and pull the bar down in a controlled arc by extending the shoulders and pulling with the lats until the bar reaches your upper thighs or mid-thigh level; keep the arms long and the elbows fixed in their slight bend.
Squeeze the lats and hold the bottom position briefly while keeping the ribs down and spine neutral.
Inhale and slowly allow the bar to rise back to the start position under control, resisting the cable so the lats remain under tension; do not let the lower back round as you return.
Repeat for the desired reps, maintaining the hip hinge, neutral spine, and long-arm position on every repetition.
exercises.description: Standing cable straight-arm pulldowns hinge from the hips and pull a high cable down with mostly straight arms to load the lats through a long-range extension. The movement emphasizes scapular depression and shoulder extension while keeping the torso braced.

exercises.name: Cable one arm lat pulldown
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height and knee pads so your feet are flat on the floor and your thighs are secured under the pads.
instructions: Adjust the seat height and knee pads so your feet are flat on the floor and your thighs are secured under the pads.
Attach a single D-handle to the high pulley. Sit upright with a slight natural arch in the lower back and grasp the handle with one hand using a neutral or overhand grip.
Start with your working arm fully extended overhead and your torso upright (a very slight lean back is optional), opposite hand resting on your thigh or the machine for stability.
Exhale and pull the handle straight down and slightly toward the side of your ribcage by driving your elbow down and back; lead the movement with the elbow rather than the hand.
At the bottom of the movement, pause and squeeze the lat and upper-back muscles, keeping the shoulder blade pulled down and back; avoid shrugging the shoulder up.
Inhale and slowly return the handle to the start position with a controlled motion, allowing the lat to stretch but maintaining tension in the cable.
Perform the desired number of reps, then switch sides. Avoid twisting the torso or using momentum — keep the movement controlled and driven by the lat.
exercises.description: A single-arm cable pulldown performed from a seated position to isolate each lat muscle while limiting trunk rotation. The exercise pulls a handle from overhead down toward the side of the chest, emphasizing a strong squeeze of the lats.

exercises.name: Cable Shrug
exercises.notes: Stand facing the cable machine with a handle attached to a low or mid pulley; feet shoulder-width apart and weight evenly distributed.
instructions: Stand facing the cable machine with a handle attached to a low or mid pulley; feet shoulder-width apart and weight evenly distributed.
Grasp one or two handles with a neutral (palms facing the body) or overhand grip and let your arms hang fully extended at your sides; keep a neutral neck and spine.
Slightly bend the knees and brace your core to stabilize the torso; do not lean forward or backward.
Exhale and elevate (shrug) your shoulders straight up toward your ears, focusing on contracting the trapezius—do not roll or rotate the shoulders.
Hold the peak contraction briefly (about 1 second) while maintaining straight arms and a neutral neck.
Inhale and slowly lower the shoulders back down to the start in a controlled manner until traps are fully stretched.
Repeat for the desired reps, keeping movement strict and avoiding momentum; reduce weight if you must use arm or torso movement to lift.
exercises.description: Standing cable shrugs target the trapezius by elevating the shoulders against resistance from a cable. Performed with arms straight at the sides, the movement emphasizes a vertical shrug with controlled lowering.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

warmup.notes: Brief warmup to activate target muscles. Can skip if first working set feels warm.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips, push the butt back, and bend the knees slightly so the torso is angled forward (near parallel to the floor), keeping a neutral spine and head in line with the spine.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and the barbell over the midfoot; grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width using an overhand or mixed grip.
Hinge at the hips, push the butt back, and bend the knees slightly so the torso is angled forward (near parallel to the floor), keeping a neutral spine and head in line with the spine.
Brace your core and retract your shoulder blades slightly to set the upper back before the first rep.
Pull the barbell upward toward the lower chest/upper abdomen by driving the elbows back and keeping them close to the body; focus on squeezing the upper back at the top of the movement.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining a flat back, then lower the barbell under control until arms are nearly extended while preserving the hip-hinge position.
Breathe out while pulling the bar up and inhale while lowering it; keep the torso stable and avoid rounding the lower back or using excessive torso momentum.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, and reset your hip-hinge position between sets if you lose posture.
exercises.description: A hip-hinged barbell row that trains the upper back by pulling the loaded barbell toward the torso while maintaining a neutral spine. It also recruits the lats, arms and posterior chain for stability and power.

exercises.name: Barbell Reverse Grip Incline Bench Row
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and position it so your chest rests firmly on the pad with feet braced on the floor or foot supports (prone position).
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and position it so your chest rests firmly on the pad with feet braced on the floor or foot supports (prone position).
Load a barbell and grasp it with a shoulder-width reverse (underhand) grip so palms face up and arms hang straight down toward the floor.
Start with your chest pressed into the bench, shoulders relaxed and scapulae slightly protracted, core engaged and neck neutral.

Initiate the movement by retracting your scapulae and pulling the barbell upward in a controlled path toward your lower chest / upper abdomen, leading with the elbows and keeping them close to your sides.
Pause 1 second at the top and squeeze the lats and upper back without hyperextending the spine or lifting your chest off the bench.
Lower the barbell slowly back to the start with full arm extension while maintaining tension in the lats; avoid dropping the weight or using momentum.
Repeat for the desired reps. Breathe out while pulling and inhale while lowering.
exercises.description: Prone incline bench row performed with a reverse (underhand) grip that emphasizes the lats while also engaging the upper back and arms. Lying chest-down on an incline bench, you pull a barbell up toward the lower chest/upper abdomen and lower it under control.

exercises.name: Cable Standing Row
exercises.notes: Set the cable pulley to about chest height and attach a close-grip or double-handle.
instructions: Set the cable pulley to about chest height and attach a close-grip or double-handle.
Stand facing the machine with feet hip-width apart (or a slight staggered stance for balance) and a soft bend in the knees; grasp the handle with a neutral or overhand grip.
Step back until the cable is taut and hinge slightly at the hips so your torso is upright but tilted forward a few degrees; brace your core and keep a neutral spine.
Begin the movement by pulling your shoulder blades together (scapular retraction), then drive your elbows straight back toward your sides, keeping them close to the body.
Pull until your hands reach your lower chest/upper abdomen and you feel a full contraction in the lats and upper back; avoid excessive torso lean or momentum.
Pause 0–1 seconds at the top while squeezing the shoulder blades, then slowly extend your arms back to the start under control, maintaining core tension.
Repeat for the desired reps and adjust weight so you can maintain strict form for the full set.
exercises.description: A standing cable rowing movement that targets the lats and mid/upper back by pulling a cable handle toward the torso while maintaining a braced, upright stance. Emphasizes scapular retraction and controlled concentric and eccentric phases.

exercises.name: Cable seated row
exercises.notes: Initiate the movement by retracting your shoulder blades, then drive your elbows straight back keeping them close to your body while pulling the handle toward your lower sternum/upper abdomen.
instructions: Sit on the bench facing the cable machine with feet braced on the footplate and knees slightly bent (seated, upright position).
Grab the handle (V-bar or close grip) with a neutral or overhand grip and extend your arms so the weight stack is lifted and shoulders are protracted; maintain a neutral spine and chest slightly up.
Initiate the movement by retracting your shoulder blades, then drive your elbows straight back keeping them close to your body while pulling the handle toward your lower sternum/upper abdomen.
Keep the torso stable—avoid excessive leaning or using momentum; maintain a slight forward trunk angle only if required to reach the handle, then return to a tall, braced posture as you pull.

Squeeze the shoulder blades and lats at the end range for a brief 1–2 second pause, then slowly extend the arms forward under control to the start position while allowing the scapulae to move forward.
Breathe out while pulling and inhale while returning to the start; choose a weight that allows controlled form and full elbow extension between reps.
exercises.description: Seated cable row is a horizontal pulling exercise performed sitting with feet braced that primarily targets the lats while working the middle/upper back and arm pulling muscles. It emphasizes scapular retraction and elbow drive to pull the handle to the torso.

exercises.name: Cable Seated One Arm Alternate Row
exercises.notes: Step 1: Set a single handle on a low pulley and sit on a bench facing the machine with feet flat and slightly braced on the floor or foot platform.
instructions: Step 1: Set a single handle on a low pulley and sit on a bench facing the machine with feet flat and slightly braced on the floor or foot platform.

Step 2: Sit upright with a neutral spine, chest slightly up, shoulders relaxed, and core braced; reach forward to grab the handle with one hand while the opposite hand can rest on your thigh for stability.
Step 3: Start with the working arm fully extended and the shoulder gently protracted (forward) so there is mild tension in the cable.
Step 4: Pull the handle back by driving the elbow straight toward your hip, focusing on squeezing the shoulder blade toward the spine (scapular retraction) and keeping the elbow close to your body.
Step 5: Maintain a stable torso — avoid rotating or leaning excessively; use the arm and back to move the weight, not body momentum.
Step 6: Pause briefly at the end of the concentric phase with a full squeeze of the lats and upper back, then slowly return the handle to the starting position under control.
Step 7: Breathe out while pulling and inhale while returning. Complete the desired reps on one side, then switch arms and repeat, alternating sides for each set.
exercises.description: A seated unilateral cable row that emphasizes the latissimus dorsi through a controlled elbow-driven pull while promoting scapular retraction; performed alternating one arm at a time to address side-to-side imbalances.

exercises.name: Cable one arm lat pulldown
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height and knee pads so your feet are flat on the floor and your thighs are secured under the pads.
instructions: Adjust the seat height and knee pads so your feet are flat on the floor and your thighs are secured under the pads.
Attach a single D-handle to the high pulley. Sit upright with a slight natural arch in the lower back and grasp the handle with one hand using a neutral or overhand grip.
Start with your working arm fully extended overhead and your torso upright (a very slight lean back is optional), opposite hand resting on your thigh or the machine for stability.
Exhale and pull the handle straight down and slightly toward the side of your ribcage by driving your elbow down and back; lead the movement with the elbow rather than the hand.
At the bottom of the movement, pause and squeeze the lat and upper-back muscles, keeping the shoulder blade pulled down and back; avoid shrugging the shoulder up.
Inhale and slowly return the handle to the start position with a controlled motion, allowing the lat to stretch but maintaining tension in the cable.
Perform the desired number of reps, then switch sides. Avoid twisting the torso or using momentum — keep the movement controlled and driven by the lat.
exercises.description: A single-arm cable pulldown performed from a seated position to isolate each lat muscle while limiting trunk rotation. The exercise pulls a handle from overhead down toward the side of the chest, emphasizing a strong squeeze of the lats.

exercises.name: Cable Neutral Grip Lat Pulldown
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height and thigh pads so your feet are flat and your thighs are secured under the pads while seated upright.
instructions: Adjust the seat height and thigh pads so your feet are flat and your thighs are secured under the pads while seated upright.
Attach a neutral (parallel) handle and grasp it with both hands so your palms face each other; sit tall with a slight lean back (~10–20°), chest up, and shoulders down and back.
Start with your arms fully extended overhead and shoulder blades slightly protracted; brace your core to keep the torso stable.
Initiate the movement by driving your elbows down and back, pulling the handle toward your upper chest / clavicle region while keeping your wrists neutral.
Squeeze your lats and pinch your shoulder blades together at the bottom of the movement for a brief 1‑2 second hold.
Return the handle slowly and under control to the start position, allowing your arms to fully extend and your shoulder blades to re‑protract.
Repeat for the desired reps, avoiding excessive torso lean, momentum, or shrugging the shoulders; keep the movement smooth and elbow-driven.
If you feel the biceps doing all the work, reduce the weight and focus on leading with the elbows and actively contracting the lats.
exercises.description: Seated cable pulldown using a neutral (parallel) grip that emphasizes the latissimus dorsi while reducing stress on the shoulders; the bar is pulled down toward the upper chest with elbow-driven movement. Performed seated with thighs braced under pads, focusing on a controlled eccentric return.

exercises.name: Barbell Shrug
exercises.notes: Stand upright (neutral spine) with feet hip- to shoulder-width apart and knees slightly soft.
instructions: Stand upright (neutral spine) with feet hip- to shoulder-width apart and knees slightly soft.
Hold a loaded barbell with a shoulder-width, pronated (overhand) grip so the bar rests behind your thighs at arm's length.
Keep arms straight (no elbow bend) and head/neck in a neutral position throughout the set.
Elevate the shoulders straight up toward the ears by contracting the traps; avoid rolling or rotating the shoulders.
Pause and squeeze the top of the contraction for 1–2 seconds, focusing on the traps.
Lower the shoulders back down slowly and under control until arms are fully extended and traps are relaxed.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions while maintaining upright posture and minimal torso movement.
exercises.description: Standing barbell shrugs isolate the trapezius by elevating the shoulders while holding a loaded barbell. The movement focuses on a vertical shrugging motion with minimal elbow or torso movement.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Standing Military Press
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart and knees slightly bent (soft), grip the barbell just wider than shoulder-width with palms facing forward.
instructions: Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart and knees slightly bent (soft), grip the barbell just wider than shoulder-width with palms facing forward.
Hold the bar at upper-chest / front-shoulder level (resting on front delts / clavicle area), elbows pointed slightly forward and directly under the wrists.
Brace your core and pull your shoulder blades down and back to stabilize the spine; keep a neutral head and avoid excessive arching of the lower back.
Inhale, then press the barbell straight up overhead by extending the elbows and driving through the delts; move the bar slightly back only enough to end with it stacked over mid-foot at full lockout.
Exhale at the top with arms fully extended but avoid hyperextending the elbows and lower back; keep the ribcage down and core tight.
Lower the bar under control back to the starting position at the upper chest, keeping wrists neutral and elbows tracking under the bar.
Repeat for desired reps. Use a spotter or lighter weight if not using a rack and maintain strict form to protect shoulders and spine.
exercises.description: A standing barbell press performed from the front shoulder/chest area to overhead, primarily working the shoulder delts while pressing the weight up in a vertical path. The lift requires core stabilization and arm extension to lock out overhead.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Overhead Press
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent, and core braced (standing position).
instructions: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent, and core braced (standing position).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with palms facing forward or neutral and elbows slightly in front of the body.
Keep the spine neutral and ribcage down to avoid hyperextending the lower back; squeeze the abs throughout.
Exhale and press both dumbbells upward in a controlled path until the arms are nearly fully extended overhead without locking the elbows.
Pause briefly at the top with shoulders stacked over hips and the head neutral (you may slightly move your head back to allow the dumbbells to pass), keeping shoulder blades stable.
Inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly back to the starting shoulder-height position under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, avoiding excessive torso lean or lower-back arching; if needed, use a lighter weight to maintain good form.
exercises.description: A standing dumbbell overhead press is a compound shoulder exercise where you press dumbbells from shoulder height to full arm extension overhead, primarily working the delts while engaging the triceps and upper back for support and stabilization.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Rear Delt Fly
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips, push your butt back and keep a slight bend in the knees until your torso is nearly parallel to the floor; maintain a neutral spine and neck (bent-over position).
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart holding a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral (palms facing each other) grip.
Hinge at the hips, push your butt back and keep a slight bend in the knees until your torso is nearly parallel to the floor; maintain a neutral spine and neck (bent-over position).
Let the arms hang straight down from the shoulders with a small bend in the elbows; keep the shoulder blades relaxed but ready to retract.
Exhale and raise the dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc (horizontal abduction) leading with the elbows until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor and at shoulder height.
Squeeze the rear delts and pinch the shoulder blades together at the top for a brief pause.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position with control, avoiding momentum or torso swing.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, keeping movement controlled and the torso stable throughout.
exercises.description: A bent-over dumbbell fly that isolates the posterior (rear) deltoids by horizontally abducting the arms from a hinged position. Performed with a hip hinge and slight elbow bend, the movement emphasizes the rear delts while engaging the upper back and traps to stabilize and retract the shoulder blades.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Standing Wide Military Press
exercises.notes: Stand upright (not kneeling or seated) with feet about shoulder-width apart and toes slightly turned out for stability.
instructions: Stand upright (not kneeling or seated) with feet about shoulder-width apart and toes slightly turned out for stability.
Approach the barbell racked at about upper-chest height; grasp the bar with a wide, overhand grip (wider than shoulder-width) so elbows will flare slightly out to the sides.
Unrack the bar and hold it at the upper-chest/collarbone level with wrists stacked over elbows and the bar resting roughly over the front of the shoulders.
Brace your core and glutes, keep a neutral spine (avoid excessive lumbar arching), and take a full breath before initiating the press.
Press the bar in a straight or slightly curved path overhead by extending the elbows and driving the bar up until the arms are fully extended and the bar is roughly above or slightly behind the head.
Hold briefly with the shoulders active and scapulae stabilized (do not shrug aggressively), then inhale and slowly lower the bar back down under control to the starting position at the upper chest.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, maintaining a tight core and neutral spine on every rep; if balance is lost, reduce weight or narrow the grip slightly.
Safety notes: avoid locking the neck forward or leaning the torso excessively back at the top; use a spotter or rack the bar between sets if needed.
exercises.description: A standing barbell overhead press performed with a wider-than-shoulder grip that emphasizes the shoulder deltoids while pressing the barbell from the upper chest to full arm extension overhead. The lift requires core bracing and scapular stability to control the bar path.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Shoulder Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with elbows bent ~90°, palms facing forward or slightly inward and wrists neutral.
Inhale, keep the spine neutral and shoulders down away from the ears.
Exhale and press both dumbbells upward in a controlled motion until the arms are nearly fully extended overhead (avoid locking the elbows).
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the delts and keeping the torso stable.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to shoulder height with control to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining a steady tempo and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Use a lighter weight or a spotter if form breaks down or if pressing heavy loads.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell shoulder press is a compound pressing movement performed from a seated, back‑supported position that primarily develops the delts while also engaging the triceps and upper back for stabilization.

exercises.name: Cable Standing Face Pull
exercises.notes: Set a cable pulley to about eye- to upper-chest height and attach a rope handle.
instructions: Set a cable pulley to about eye- to upper-chest height and attach a rope handle.
Stand facing the machine with feet shoulder-width apart or in a slight staggered stance, knees soft and spine neutral.
Grasp the rope with both hands using a neutral/overhand grip (palms facing inward) and take a step back so there is tension on the cable.
Initiate the movement by retracting the shoulder blades and pulling the rope toward your face, driving the elbows out to the sides so your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor.
As you reach the end range, externally rotate the shoulders so the hands split apart and the rope ends are near your temples or ears; pause and squeeze the rear delts and upper back.
Slowly reverse the motion under control, extending the arms forward while maintaining scapular control until the hands return to the start position.
Repeat for the desired reps, keeping torso stable (no excessive leaning or momentum) and focusing on quality of movement and external rotation.
exercises.description: A standing cable face pull with a rope attachment that emphasizes the posterior delts and upper back by pulling the rope toward the face while externally rotating the shoulders. It develops rear shoulder strength, scapular retraction, and shoulder external rotators.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Rear Fly
exercises.notes: Hinge forward at the hips until your torso is nearly parallel to the floor (or a 30–45° decline), keep a neutral spine and neck, and brace your abs.
instructions: Sit on the edge of a bench with feet flat about hip-width apart and hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral grip (palms facing each other).
Hinge forward at the hips until your torso is nearly parallel to the floor (or a 30–45° decline), keep a neutral spine and neck, and brace your abs.
Allow the dumbbells to hang beneath your shoulders with a slight bend in the elbows; maintain that elbow angle throughout the set.

Exhale and lift the dumbbells out to your sides in a wide arc by externally rotating the shoulders and squeezing the shoulder blades together; raise until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor.
Pause briefly at the top focusing on contracting the rear delts and upper back, then inhale and lower the weights slowly back to the starting position with control.
Avoid swinging the torso or using momentum, do not shrug the shoulders up toward the ears, and keep the movement driven by the shoulders/scapulae rather than the lower back.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell rear fly is a bent-over horizontal shoulder abduction movement that targets the rear delts and upper back by pulling the arms out to the sides while keeping the torso stable. It strengthens posterior shoulder muscles and scapular retractors with a controlled, rowing-like motion.

exercises.name: Dumbbell One Arm Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet about hip-to-shoulder width, knees soft, and a neutral spine; hold a dumbbell in the working hand with the other hand resting on your hip or at your side for balance.
instructions: Stand upright with feet about hip-to-shoulder width, knees soft, and a neutral spine; hold a dumbbell in the working hand with the other hand resting on your hip or at your side for balance.
Start with the working arm hanging at your side, palm facing your body and a slight bend in the elbow (avoid locking the joint).
Brace your core and keep the torso still — avoid leaning or swinging the body to lift the weight.
Exhale and raise the dumbbell out to the side in a controlled arc, keeping the elbow slightly bent and the wrist neutral, until the upper arm is approximately parallel to the floor (shoulder height).
Keep the shoulder depressed (do not shrug) and lead the motion with the elbow rather than the hand; avoid rotating the torso.
Pause briefly at the top, then inhale and lower the dumbbell slowly back to the starting position under control.
Perform the desired number of repetitions, then switch arms. Choose a light-to-moderate weight to maintain strict form and avoid momentum.
exercises.description: A single-arm standing dumbbell lateral raise that isolates the lateral deltoid to widen and strengthen the shoulder; the movement lifts the arm out to the side to about shoulder height while the rest of the body remains stable.

exercises.name: Cable Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles. Stand centered between the two towers and grasp each handle with the opposite hand so the cables cross in front of you (or hold same-side handles if preferred).
Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart, knees soft, and core braced (neutral spine). Keep a slight bend in the elbows and wrists neutral.
Start with the handles low by your thighs; palms can face inward or slightly forward. Maintain a small forward lean from the hips if comfortable to allow a natural arc.
Keeping the elbows slightly bent, exhale and raise both arms out to the sides in a smooth arc until your hands reach approximately shoulder height. Lead the movement with the elbow, not the wrist.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the delts; avoid shrugging the shoulders upward toward your ears.
Inhale and lower the handles slowly and under control back to the starting position, keeping tension on the cables throughout the descent.
Repeat for the desired number of reps. Use a lighter weight if you cannot maintain strict form or if you begin using torso momentum.
exercises.description: A standing cable lateral raise performed from low pulleys to isolate the lateral head of the delts; cables provide constant tension while you lift the arms out to the sides to shoulder height. The movement is done with a slight elbow bend and controlled tempo to minimize momentum.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Standing Wide Military Press
exercises.notes: Stand upright (not kneeling or seated) with feet about shoulder-width apart and toes slightly turned out for stability.
instructions: Stand upright (not kneeling or seated) with feet about shoulder-width apart and toes slightly turned out for stability.
Approach the barbell racked at about upper-chest height; grasp the bar with a wide, overhand grip (wider than shoulder-width) so elbows will flare slightly out to the sides.
Unrack the bar and hold it at the upper-chest/collarbone level with wrists stacked over elbows and the bar resting roughly over the front of the shoulders.
Brace your core and glutes, keep a neutral spine (avoid excessive lumbar arching), and take a full breath before initiating the press.
Press the bar in a straight or slightly curved path overhead by extending the elbows and driving the bar up until the arms are fully extended and the bar is roughly above or slightly behind the head.
Hold briefly with the shoulders active and scapulae stabilized (do not shrug aggressively), then inhale and slowly lower the bar back down under control to the starting position at the upper chest.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, maintaining a tight core and neutral spine on every rep; if balance is lost, reduce weight or narrow the grip slightly.
Safety notes: avoid locking the neck forward or leaning the torso excessively back at the top; use a spotter or rack the bar between sets if needed.
exercises.description: A standing barbell overhead press performed with a wider-than-shoulder grip that emphasizes the shoulder deltoids while pressing the barbell from the upper chest to full arm extension overhead. The lift requires core bracing and scapular stability to control the bar path.

exercises.name: Barbell Seated Overhead Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with feet flat on the floor about hip-width apart and torso vertical (back neutral).
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with feet flat on the floor about hip-width apart and torso vertical (back neutral).
Grip the barbell with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, wrists stacked over elbows and palms facing forward; rest the bar at upper chest/clavicle level.
Brace your core and squeeze your shoulder blades down and slightly back to stabilize the spine; keep head neutral with eyes forward.
Inhale, then press the bar upward in a vertical path by extending your elbows and driving through the delts until arms are nearly locked out overhead.
As the bar passes your forehead, slightly tilt your head back to allow clearance, then return your head to neutral at the top.
Pause briefly at the top with the bar roughly over mid-foot, shoulder blades stable and ribs down to avoid excessive lower-back arching.
Exhale and slowly lower the bar under control back to the starting position at the upper chest/clavicle, keeping elbows slightly in front of the body.
Repeat for the desired reps, maintaining a tight core and steady breathing; avoid bouncing the bar off the chest or flaring the ribs.
exercises.description: Seated barbell overhead press performed from a seated, upright position pressing a barbell from the upper chest/clavicle to full overhead lockout. Primarily targets the shoulder delts while engaging triceps and upper traps for assistance and stabilization.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent and core braced (standing position).
instructions: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent and core braced (standing position).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a neutral grip (palms facing your body) and wrists straight.
Keep a small bend in the elbows throughout the movement and maintain a neutral spine and chest up.
Exhale and raise both arms out to the sides by lifting at the shoulders, leading with the elbows so the hands finish roughly at shoulder height (arms nearly parallel to the floor).
Avoid shrugging the shoulders up toward the ears; keep the neck long and scapulae stable to minimize excessive trap involvement.
Pause briefly (0–1 second) at the top while maintaining tension in the delts.
Inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly and controlled back to the starting position at your sides.
Repeat for desired reps, keeping movement smooth and avoiding momentum or swinging; use lighter weight if form breaks down.
exercises.description: Standing dumbbell lateral raise works the lateral delts by lifting the arms out to the sides to about shoulder height, with a slight elbow bend and controlled motion. It improves shoulder width and isolates the middle shoulder fibers while the traps and forearms assist.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Rear Delt Raise
exercises.notes: Stand feet hip-width apart holding a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral (palms facing) grip.
instructions: Stand feet hip-width apart holding a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral (palms facing) grip.
Hinge forward at the hips until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor, keep a neutral spine and knees slightly bent (bent-over position).
Let your arms hang straight down from the shoulders with a slight bend in the elbows; maintain a braced core and avoid rounding the back.
Keeping the elbows slightly bent, raise the dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the ground (level with the shoulders).
Squeeze the rear delts and shoulder blades together at the top for a brief pause, avoid shrugging the shoulders up toward the ears.
Slowly lower the dumbbells back to the start under control, resisting any momentum; repeat for desired reps.
Keep the movement strict: use lighter weight if you must swing the torso or bend the elbows excessively, and breathe out on the lift and in on the lower.
exercises.description: A bent-over dumbbell raise that isolates the rear (posterior) deltoids by lifting the arms out to the sides while the torso is hinged forward. It emphasizes shoulder horizontal abduction and scapular retraction with a controlled tempo.

exercises.name: Cable Standing Supinated Face Pull (with rope)
exercises.notes: Set a cable pulley to about eye to upper-chest height and attach a rope handle.
instructions: Set a cable pulley to about eye to upper-chest height and attach a rope handle.
Stand facing the machine in a staggered or hip-width stance with a slight bend in the knees and a neutral spine.
Reach forward and grab the rope ends with a supinated (palms-up) grip so your thumbs point toward you; step back until there is tension in the cable and your arms are nearly extended.
Brace your core, exhale, and pull the rope toward your face by driving your elbows out to the sides and externally rotating the shoulders so the hands move toward the ears.
At the end of the movement squeeze the shoulder blades together and pause 1–2 seconds while keeping the torso stable (avoid leaning back or shrugging).
Inhale and slowly extend the arms forward to the start position under control, maintaining tension in the cable.
Repeat for the desired reps, keeping tempo controlled and focusing on rear delt and upper-back contraction rather than using momentum.
exercises.description: Standing cable face pull with a supinated (palms-up) rope grip emphasizes the rear delts while bringing the shoulder blades together and externally rotating the shoulders. Pull the rope toward your face with elbows high, then return under control.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Front and Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Stand upright (standing) with feet about hip-to-shoulder width, knees soft and core braced; hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a neutral grip.
instructions: Stand upright (standing) with feet about hip-to-shoulder width, knees soft and core braced; hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a neutral grip.
Keep a small bend in the elbows and maintain a neutral wrist; shoulders down and back (do not shrug) to avoid excess trap activation.
Front raise: exhale and lift both dumbbells straight forward with controlled motion until your arms are roughly parallel to the floor (about shoulder height), palms facing down or slightly inward.
Pause briefly at the top (0–1 second), then inhale and lower the weights slowly back to the starting position under control.

Immediately perform the lateral raise: exhale and lift both dumbbells out to your sides with the same slight elbow bend, leading with the elbow, raising to shoulder height so the arms form a T with your torso.
Hold briefly at the top, then inhale and lower the weights slowly back to your sides with control, avoiding swinging or using momentum.
Repeat the sequence (front raise then lateral raise) for the desired number of repetitions, keeping core engaged and torso still throughout.

Progression/cues: use light-to-moderate weight to maintain strict form, keep movements slow (about 1–2 seconds up, 2–3 seconds down), and avoid elevating the shoulders toward the ears to limit excessive trap involvement.
exercises.description: A standing dumbbell combination that first raises the weights in front of the body (front raise) then out to the sides (lateral raise) to train the shoulder delts. Use controlled movement and a slight elbow bend to isolate the delts and minimize momentum.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Raise
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms fully extended down at your sides and palms facing inward; keep a slight bend in the elbows.
Brace your core, keep your chest up and neck neutral, and avoid arching your lower back.
Exhale and lift both dumbbells upward and slightly forward in a controlled arc until your hands reach about shoulder height (arms roughly parallel to the floor).
Pause briefly at the top, maintaining tension in the delts and avoiding momentum or swinging.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting hanging position under control.
Perform the desired repetitions, keeping movement smooth and avoiding use of torso momentum; reduce weight if you need to swing to complete reps.
exercises.description: Seated on an incline bench, this variation of the dumbbell raise emphasizes the front portion of the shoulder (anterior delts) by lifting the weights from a hanging position up to about shoulder height. The incline angle shifts some load onto the chest (pectorals) while isolating the delts and minimizing torso swing.

exercises.name: Cable Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles. Stand centered between the two towers and grasp each handle with the opposite hand so the cables cross in front of you (or hold same-side handles if preferred).
Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart, knees soft, and core braced (neutral spine). Keep a slight bend in the elbows and wrists neutral.
Start with the handles low by your thighs; palms can face inward or slightly forward. Maintain a small forward lean from the hips if comfortable to allow a natural arc.
Keeping the elbows slightly bent, exhale and raise both arms out to the sides in a smooth arc until your hands reach approximately shoulder height. Lead the movement with the elbow, not the wrist.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the delts; avoid shrugging the shoulders upward toward your ears.
Inhale and lower the handles slowly and under control back to the starting position, keeping tension on the cables throughout the descent.
Repeat for the desired number of reps. Use a lighter weight if you cannot maintain strict form or if you begin using torso momentum.
exercises.description: A standing cable lateral raise performed from low pulleys to isolate the lateral head of the delts; cables provide constant tension while you lift the arms out to the sides to shoulder height. The movement is done with a slight elbow bend and controlled tempo to minimize momentum.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Lying Close grip Press
exercises.notes: Set up: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and shoulder blades retracted; position the bar on the rack directly above your eyes.
instructions: Set up: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and shoulder blades retracted; position the bar on the rack directly above your eyes.
Grip the bar with a close grip (hands about shoulder-width or slightly narrower — roughly 6–12 inches between hands) with palms facing up and wrists stacked over elbows.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving it over your chest; keep your shoulder blades pinched and a slight arch in the upper back.
Descend: Inhale and lower the bar in a controlled manner to the mid-to-lower chest/sternum while keeping your elbows tucked toward your torso (about a 30–45° angle) to target the triceps.
Press: Exhale and press the bar upward by extending the elbows until arms are nearly straight, maintaining control and avoiding aggressive hyperextension of the elbows.
Repeat for the desired reps with a steady tempo (e.g., 2 seconds down, brief pause, 1 second up).
Safety: Use a spotter or set safety pins in the rack at chest height; keep wrists neutral and avoid flaring the elbows out wide to reduce shoulder strain.
exercises.description: A flat bench press variation performed with a narrow grip to emphasize the triceps while still recruiting the pectorals and front delts. Performed lying supine on a bench, the bar is lowered to the mid/lower chest and pressed back up with elbows kept close to the body.

exercises.name: Cable Standing One Arm Triceps Extension
exercises.notes: Set the cable to a high pulley and attach a single handle; select a light-to-moderate weight.
instructions: Set the cable to a high pulley and attach a single handle; select a light-to-moderate weight.
Stand facing the machine or slightly to the side with feet hip-width apart and a slight stagger for balance; hold the handle with a neutral or overhand grip.
Raise your working elbow so the upper arm is close to your torso and the forearm is bent; keep the elbow pointing down and fixed (standing position).
Brace your core (abs) and keep a neutral spine; avoid flaring the elbow or leaning excessively.
Exhale and extend the elbow, pressing the handle down and back until the arm is fully straight and the triceps are contracted.
Pause briefly at full extension, maintaining a stable upper arm and shoulder (delts assist in stabilization).
Inhale and slowly return the handle to the starting position with control, allowing the triceps to lengthen but keeping the elbow tucked.
Repeat for the desired repetitions, then switch arms. Maintain wrist neutrality and avoid using torso momentum.
exercises.description: A single-arm cable triceps extension performed standing that isolates the triceps by extending the elbow against a high-pulley cable. Keep the upper arm stationary and the elbow tucked to maximize triceps activation.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Hammer Preacher Curl
exercises.notes: Hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral/hammer grip (palms facing each other, thumbs pointing up) and let your arms hang fully extended to establish the start position.
instructions: Sit on a preacher bench with your chest against the pad and feet flat on the floor; rest the back of your upper arms on the angled pad so elbows are slightly forward of the bench edge.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral/hammer grip (palms facing each other, thumbs pointing up) and let your arms hang fully extended to establish the start position.
Brace your core, keep shoulders down and retracted, and ensure the upper arms remain pressed to the pad throughout the movement.

Exhale and curl the dumbbells by flexing only at the elbows, driving the forearms upward while maintaining the neutral grip; lift until the dumbbells approach shoulder level or you reach a full comfortable contraction.
Pause briefly at the top and squeeze the forearm muscles, then inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells in a controlled manner back to full extension without lifting the upper arms off the pad.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, avoiding body swing, shoulder involvement, or wrist bending; choose a weight that allows strict form.
exercises.description: Seated preacher-style hammer curl using dumbbells with a neutral (thumbs-up) grip to isolate the forearms (brachioradialis) while limiting shoulder movement. The angled pad supports the upper arms so the elbows stay fixed and the movement comes from elbow flexion.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.
