exercises.name: Barbell JM-Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up supine on a flat bench with feet planted on the floor, eyes roughly under the bar; use a spotter or safety pins if available.
instructions: Set up supine on a flat bench with feet planted on the floor, eyes roughly under the bar; use a spotter or safety pins if available.
Grip the bar with a narrow to shoulder-width grip (hands about shoulder-width or slightly narrower) and unrack the bar, arms extended over chest.
Keeping the elbows tucked but not pinned to the sides, slowly lower the bar in a slight arc toward the area between the forehead and upper chest, bending only at the elbows.
Stop the descent when the forearms are near the forehead/upper chest (comfortable range) and pause briefly without bouncing.
Drive the bar back up by extending the elbows, following the same slight arc until the arms are fully extended over the chest; avoid flaring the elbows outward.
Control the eccentric (lowering) and concentric (lifting) phases, breathe in on the descent and exhale as you press up.
Rack the bar under control at the top or have the spotter assist when finished; use light weight and strict form to protect the elbows and shoulders.
exercises.description: A lying barbell press that blends a close-grip bench press and skullcrusher motion to emphasize elbow extension; the bar is lowered in a slight arc toward the forehead/upper-chest area and pressed back up. Primarily trains the triceps while also recruiting the pectorals and front delts for stabilization and drive.

exercises.name: Barbell Lying Triceps Extension Skull Crusher
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet planted on the floor for stability and your head near the end of the bench so the bar can be lowered toward your forehead.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet planted on the floor for stability and your head near the end of the bench so the bar can be lowered toward your forehead.
Grip the barbell with a shoulder-width (or slightly narrower) overhand grip and press it up so your arms are fully extended above your chest; wrists should be neutral and stacked over elbows.
Keep your upper arms fixed and perpendicular to the floor (or slightly angled toward your head) throughout the set — only the forearm should move.
Inhale and slowly lower the bar by flexing the elbows, bringing the bar toward your forehead or just above your temples in a controlled arc.
Pause briefly when the bar is a few inches from your forehead (avoid actually hitting your head) and maintain tension in the triceps.
Exhale and extend the elbows to press the bar back up to the start position, locking out but avoiding hyperextension of the elbows.
Keep wrists straight and avoid flaring the elbows outward or letting the upper arms drift; use a spotter if loading heavy for safety.
Perform repetitions with slow, controlled tempo and stop the set if you feel shoulder or wrist pain — reduce weight if form breaks down.
exercises.description: A supine barbell isolation exercise that works elbow extension to load the triceps by lowering the bar toward the forehead (skull crusher) and extending back up. Emphasizes controlled movement from the elbow while keeping the upper arms relatively stationary.

exercises.name: Cable Triceps Pushdown (SZ bar)
exercises.notes: Attach an SZ (curl) bar to the high pulley of a cable machine and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Attach an SZ (curl) bar to the high pulley of a cable machine and select an appropriate weight.
Stand facing the machine with feet about shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent and core braced (upright torso).
Grab the SZ bar with a neutral/overhand grip (palms facing down or slightly toward you), hands about shoulder-width apart.
Start with the bar at chest/upper-ab level, elbows tucked close to your sides and upper arms stationary (forearms up).
Exhale and press the bar down by extending your elbows until your arms are fully straight and triceps are contracted; keep wrists neutral and avoid shrugging or leaning forward.
Pause briefly at full extension, then inhale and slowly allow the bar to rise back to the starting position by bending the elbows while maintaining upper-arm position.
Repeat for the desired number of reps while keeping movement controlled and avoiding momentum or excessive torso movement.
exercises.description: Standing cable pushdown using an SZ (curl) bar that emphasizes elbow extension to work the triceps; performed facing the cable machine with the upper arms held stationary at the sides. Keep a neutral spine and controlled tempo, extending the arms fully and returning under control.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Kickback
exercises.notes: Hinge forward at the hips until your torso is nearly parallel to the floor, maintain a neutral spine and brace your abs.
instructions: Position yourself with one knee and the same-side hand resting on a flat bench (all fours variation with one leg off the bench); place the opposite foot on the floor for stability.
Hinge forward at the hips until your torso is nearly parallel to the floor, maintain a neutral spine and brace your abs.
Hold a dumbbell in the free hand with a neutral grip (palm facing your torso) and let your upper arm hang so the elbow is bent about 90° and tucked close to your side.
Keep the upper arm stationary and exhale as you extend your elbow, driving the dumbbell straight back until your arm is fully extended and the triceps is contracted.
Pause briefly at full extension, focusing on squeezing the triceps, then inhale and slowly return the dumbbell to the starting 90° elbow position with control.
Perform the desired repetitions, then switch sides. Avoid swinging your torso or using momentum — only the forearm should move.
exercises.description: Single-arm dumbbell kickback performed with one hand and knee supported on a bench; extend the elbow to drive the dumbbell back and straighten the arm, emphasizing the triceps. Keep the torso stable and hinge at the hips so only the forearm moves.

exercises.name: EZ Barbell Curl
exercises.notes: Stand upright (weight-bearing, standing position) with feet hip-width apart and knees soft, holding an EZ curl bar with an underhand/angled grip (hands on the inner grips, about shoulder width).
instructions: Stand upright (weight-bearing, standing position) with feet hip-width apart and knees soft, holding an EZ curl bar with an underhand/angled grip (hands on the inner grips, about shoulder width).
Keep your chest up, shoulders back and down, core braced (engage abs) and elbows pinned to your sides; wrists should follow the bar's natural angle.
Exhale and curl the bar by flexing your elbows, keeping the upper arms stationary and driving the forearms toward the shoulders until the biceps are fully contracted.
Pause for a brief 1 second at the top and squeeze the biceps, making sure not to use momentum or swing the torso.
Inhale and slowly lower the bar back to the starting position with a controlled eccentric motion until arms are almost fully extended but not locked out.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions. Maintain strict form—avoid leaning back or letting the elbows drift forward to ensure biceps remain the primary movers.
exercises.description: Standing curl using an EZ curl bar to isolate elbow flexion and emphasize the biceps while the angled bar reduces wrist strain. Performed with a supinated/angled grip and controlled concentric and eccentric phases.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Zottman Curl
exercises.notes: Stand upright (not leaning), feet hip-width apart, knees soft and core braced; hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with palms facing forward (supinated).
instructions: Stand upright (not leaning), feet hip-width apart, knees soft and core braced; hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with palms facing forward (supinated).
Keep your upper arms stationary and close to your torso, then exhale and curl the dumbbells upward by flexing your elbows until the dumbbells reach shoulder level.
At the top of the curl, pause briefly and rotate both wrists so your palms turn to face downward (pronated).
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back down to your sides with palms facing down, maintaining control and avoiding momentum.
Once the dumbbells reach the bottom, rotate your wrists back to the palms-up (supinated) starting position and repeat for the desired reps.
Keep movements controlled, avoid swinging the torso, and breathe out on the lift and in on the lowering phase.
exercises.description: A standing dumbbell curl that combines a supinated concentric curl with a pronated eccentric lowering to work the biceps on the lift and the forearms during the rotation and lowering phase. Emphasizes controlled rotation of the wrist at the top of the movement.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Curl
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet about shoulder-width apart, knees soft, and core braced (standing position).
instructions: Stand upright with feet about shoulder-width apart, knees soft, and core braced (standing position).
Hold the barbell with a shoulder-width underhand (supinated) grip so your palms face forward and the bar rests on the base of the fingers.
Start with your arms fully extended down at your thighs, elbows tucked close to your torso and wrists neutral (no excessive bending).
Exhale and curl the bar upward by flexing the elbows, keeping the upper arms and elbows stationary at your sides — lift until the forearms approach the biceps or the bar is near chest level.
Pause briefly at the top and squeeze the biceps, avoiding shoulder shrugging or leaning back (no trunk momentum).
Inhale and slowly lower the bar back to the start position with a controlled eccentric motion until the arms are fully extended.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, maintaining steady tempo and strict form; reduce weight if you cannot keep elbows stable and avoid swinging the torso.
exercises.description: Standing barbell curl performed with a shoulder-width, underhand (supinated) grip; the exercise isolates elbow flexion to work the biceps with the forearms and delts assisting for stabilization. Use a controlled concentric lift and slow eccentric lowering for full muscle stimulation.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Kickback
exercises.notes: Place one knee and the same-side hand on a flat bench (all fours style) with the opposite foot on the floor for support; hold a dumbbell in the free hand with a neutral grip.
instructions: Place one knee and the same-side hand on a flat bench (all fours style) with the opposite foot on the floor for support; hold a dumbbell in the free hand with a neutral grip.
Hinge at the hips so your torso is nearly parallel to the floor, keep your spine neutral and core braced.
Start with the working elbow bent ~90° and upper arm aligned with your torso, keeping the upper arm fixed and close to your side.
Exhale and extend the forearm back by straightening the elbow until the arm is fully extended and the triceps are contracted; avoid rotating the shoulder or swinging the torso.
Pause briefly at full extension, then inhale and slowly lower the dumbbell back to the starting 90° elbow angle with control.
Perform the desired number of repetitions, then switch sides. Use a lighter weight if you cannot maintain a still upper arm or neutral spine.
exercises.description: A single-arm, bent-over dumbbell extension performed with the torso supported on a bench to isolate the triceps by extending the elbow. The movement emphasizes a strict elbow extension while keeping the upper arm stationary to maximize triceps contraction.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing French Press
exercises.notes: Stand tall in a neutral, upright position with feet about shoulder-width apart and knees slightly soft (not locked).
instructions: Stand tall in a neutral, upright position with feet about shoulder-width apart and knees slightly soft (not locked).
Grip a single dumbbell with both hands (palms supporting one end) and lift it overhead until your arms are extended.
Keep your upper arms (elbows) pointing up and close to your head throughout the movement; brace your abs and maintain a neutral spine.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbell behind your head by bending only at the elbows until your forearms are roughly parallel to the floor or you feel a comfortable stretch in the triceps.
Pause briefly at the bottom while keeping the elbows stationary and upper arms vertical.
Exhale and press the weight back up by extending the elbows until the arms are nearly straight; avoid locking the elbows at the top.
Perform controlled repetitions, keeping the shoulders (delts) and forearms stable and the core engaged to prevent leaning or arching.
If using heavier weight causes the elbows to flare or the torso to rock, reduce the load or perform the exercise seated for additional stability.
exercises.description: A standing overhead triceps extension performed with a single dumbbell held behind the head to isolate and strengthen the triceps while the shoulders and core stabilize the movement.

exercises.name: Cable Standing One Arm Triceps Extension
exercises.notes: Attach a single D-handle to the high pulley and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Attach a single D-handle to the high pulley and select an appropriate weight.
Stand facing slightly away from the machine in a stable, standing position with feet hip-width or staggered for balance.
Grasp the handle with one hand, keep the elbow tucked close to your side and flexed so the forearm is angled upward or slightly forward (standing position).
Brace your core and keep your upper arm stationary (minimal shoulder movement) throughout the set.
Extend the elbow by pressing the handle down and back until the arm is fully straight and the triceps are contracted; keep the wrist neutral.
Slowly return the handle to the starting position with a controlled eccentric motion until the forearm is back to the initial bent position.
Complete desired repetitions, then switch arms and repeat the same setup and technique on the other side.
exercises.description: A single-arm cable exercise performed standing that isolates the triceps by extending the elbow against a high-pulley resistance. The movement emphasizes a controlled concentric extension and slow eccentric return while the upper arm stays stable.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Single Arm Overhead Triceps Extension
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and core engaged to maintain a neutral spine.
instructions: Sit upright on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and core engaged to maintain a neutral spine.
Pick up a dumbbell with one hand and press it overhead until your arm is fully extended; keep the wrist neutral and elbow pointing forward/up.
Optionally place your free hand on the working upper arm or elbow for additional support and to help keep the upper arm vertical.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbell by flexing the elbow, bringing the weight down behind your head until your forearm is roughly parallel to the floor or you feel a stretch in the triceps.
Keep the upper arm stationary and avoid excessive leaning or flaring of the elbow; only the forearm should move.
Exhale and press the dumbbell back up by extending the elbow until the arm is straight but not locked out aggressively.
Repeat for the desired reps, then switch sides. Use a controlled tempo and avoid using momentum or excessive trunk movement.
exercises.description: Seated single-arm overhead triceps extension isolates the triceps by extending the elbow with a dumbbell held overhead while the upper arm remains stationary. It is performed one arm at a time to address unilateral strength and imbalance.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Lying Extension (across face)
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and a neutral spine.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and a neutral spine.

Hold a single dumbbell in one hand (or one in each hand for both arms) with a neutral or slightly pronated grip and bring the upper arm vertical so the elbow sits above or slightly behind the shoulder.
Start with the elbow flexed and the dumbbell near the forehead/face (across the face position) while keeping the upper arm fixed; the movement should come from the elbow joint only.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbell toward the forehead/face (or slightly behind the head depending on comfort) by bending the elbow, keeping the upper arm stationary and the wrist neutral.
Pause briefly at the bottom with tension on the triceps, then exhale and contract the triceps to extend the elbow, pushing the weight back to full elbow extension above the shoulder.
Avoid flaring the elbow excessively, using momentum, or arching the lower back; keep movement controlled and the shoulder stable throughout.
Perform the desired number of reps on one side, then switch arms if training single-arm, or continue alternating if using both arms simultaneously.
exercises.description: A supine single-arm or alternating dumbbell triceps extension performed on a flat bench, lowering the weight toward the forehead/face and extending the elbow to work the triceps. Maintain a stable upper arm and move the forearm through the range of motion with controlled tempo.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Alternate Biceps Curl
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart, knees soft and core braced (standing position).
instructions: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart, knees soft and core braced (standing position). Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with palms facing forward (supinated grip).
Keep your elbows fixed close to your torso and shoulders down; do not let elbows drift forward. Maintain a neutral wrist (no excessive bending).
Exhale and curl the right dumbbell upward by flexing the elbow until the dumbbell reaches shoulder level; squeeze the biceps at the top. Keep the upper arm stationary — only the forearm should move.
Inhale and slowly lower the right dumbbell back to the starting position with a controlled eccentric motion.
Repeat the same curl pattern on the left arm while the right arm rests at your side, then alternate sides in a smooth, controlled rhythm (alternate repetitions).
Avoid using momentum or swinging the torso; if you find yourself swinging, reduce the weight. Continue to breathe steadily: exhale on each curl, inhale on each lower.
exercises.description: Standing alternate dumbbell biceps curls performed one arm at a time to isolate elbow flexion and build the biceps. Performed with controlled concentric curls and slow eccentric lowering while keeping the torso stable.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Drag Biceps Curl
exercises.notes: Stand tall (upright) with feet hip-width apart holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides; start with palms facing your torso (neutral grip) and arms fully extended.
instructions: Stand tall (upright) with feet hip-width apart holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides; start with palms facing your torso (neutral grip) and arms fully extended.
Keep your chest up, core braced and shoulders down; avoid leaning back or swinging the torso throughout the movement.
Initiate the rep by pulling your elbows backwards (toward your hips) and driving them up, dragging the dumbbells straight up close to your body so the weights travel along the front of your torso.
Rotate the forearms as needed so the palms end up facing your shoulders at the top; squeeze the biceps at peak contraction and pause briefly.
Lower the dumbbells slowly by allowing the elbows to move forward and extending the arms back to the starting position under control.
Repeat for desired reps while keeping motion smooth and avoiding momentum from the shoulders or back.
exercises.description: A standing curl variation where the dumbbells are 'dragged' up the body by driving the elbows backwards to emphasize biceps peak contraction while minimizing shoulder involvement. The weights stay close to the torso as you lift and lower under control.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Squat
exercises.notes: Set the bar in a squat rack at about mid-chest height.
instructions: Set the bar in a squat rack at about mid-chest height. Position yourself under the bar so it rests across your upper traps (high-bar) or rear delts (low-bar) depending on preference.
Grip the bar with hands just outside shoulder width, elbows pointing down and chest up. Stand with feet roughly shoulder-width apart and toes pointed slightly outward.
Brace your core and take a full breath in, then unrack the bar by extending your legs and taking 1–2 controlled steps backward to clear the rack; establish a stable, shoulder-width stance.
Keep a neutral spine and an upright chest. Initiate the movement by pushing your hips back slightly and bending at the knees, sending your knees outward to track over your toes.
Descend in a controlled manner until your thighs are at least parallel to the floor (or deeper if mobility allows) while keeping weight on your midfoot/heels and maintaining a braced core.
Avoid knee collapse (valgus) — consciously push knees outward and keep toes engaged. Do not let the lower back round; maintain a neutral spine throughout.
Drive through your heels and midfoot to stand back up, exhaling as you ascend and extending hips and knees simultaneously until fully upright.
Reset your breathing and bracing between reps as needed. When finished, step forward under control and re-rack the bar securely.
Use appropriate weight, a spotter or safety pins in the rack if lifting near maximum loads, and adjust stance or bar position to reduce discomfort in shoulders or lower back.
exercises.description: Barbell back squat is a compound, standing lower-body exercise that primarily develops the quadriceps while also engaging the glutes, hamstrings and core for stability. The barbell rests across the upper back while you descend by bending hips and knees, then drive back up to standing.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Front Squat
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet about shoulder-width apart and toes pointed slightly outward, holding a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height (front rack position) with elbows lifted and palms facing inward or upward.
instructions: Stand tall with feet about shoulder-width apart and toes pointed slightly outward, holding a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height (front rack position) with elbows lifted and palms facing inward or upward.
Brace your core, keep your chest up and shoulders back so the torso stays upright throughout the movement.
Initiate the movement by bending at the hips and knees simultaneously, sending hips back slightly while lowering straight down as if sitting into a chair.
Keep knees tracking over the toes (don’t let them collapse inward) and lower until your thighs are about parallel to the floor or slightly below, maintaining high elbows and a neutral spine.
Pause briefly at the bottom while maintaining tension in the quads and core.
Drive through your heels and mid-foot to extend the hips and knees, returning to the starting standing position while exhaling.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, controlling both the descent and ascent and avoiding forward lean or rounding of the lower back.
exercises.description: A front-loaded squat variation performed holding dumbbells at the shoulders; emphasizes the quadriceps while also engaging the glutes, hamstrings and core. Maintain an upright torso and high elbows as you descend into a controlled squat to about parallel.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Single Leg Calf Raise
exercises.notes: Stand upright holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides (or one dumbbell in each hand if preferred), feet hip-width apart.
instructions: Stand upright holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides (or one dumbbell in each hand if preferred), feet hip-width apart.

Shift your weight onto one foot and lift the opposite foot slightly off the floor so you are balancing on a single leg (standing position, single-leg). Keep the supporting knee straight but not locked and torso tall.
Brace your core and maintain a neutral spine; if needed lightly touch a wall or hold a sturdy object for balance (wall-assisted) without leaning.

Slowly raise your heel by plantarflexing the ankle of the supporting leg until you are on the ball of your foot/toes, squeezing the calf at the top. Keep shoulders relaxed and dumbbells at your sides.
Pause 1–2 seconds at the top while maintaining balance and control.
Lower your heel slowly back to the starting position until the sole is flat on the floor (or slightly below the step edge if using a raised surface for increased range of motion).
Complete the desired number of repetitions on that leg, then switch to the other leg and repeat.
exercises.description: A single-leg standing calf raise with dumbbells isolates the calf (plantarflexors) on one side while challenging balance and stability. It builds strength and size in the calves while the core, grip, and leg stabilizers assist.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

The barbell rests across the upper back while you descend by bending hips and knees, then drive back up to standing.

exercises.name: Barbell Front Squat
exercises.notes: Set the barbell in a front-rack position on a squat rack at about collarbone height; approach the bar and step under it so it rests on the anterior deltoids and across the clavicles in a cross-arm or clean grip.
instructions: Set the barbell in a front-rack position on a squat rack at about collarbone height; approach the bar and step under it so it rests on the anterior deltoids and across the clavicles in a cross-arm or clean grip.
Establish your grip (cross-arm shown in video) and lift the chest: elbows high and pointed forward to create a stable shelf for the bar; keep the upper back tight and shoulders engaged.
Unrack the bar by standing up and taking 1-2 steps back; position feet shoulder-width apart with toes turned slightly outward and weight distributed on the midfoot/heel.
Brace your core and inhale deeply to create intra-abdominal pressure; keep the chest up and eyes forward throughout the movement.
Initiate the squat by bending at the hips and knees simultaneously, sitting back slightly but maintaining an upright torso so the bar stays balanced over the mid-foot.

Descend until the thighs are at least parallel to the floor (or to a comfortable depth while maintaining an upright torso and neutral spine). Ensure knees track in line with the toes and do not cave inward.
Drive the movement upward by pressing through the heels and midfoot, extending the knees and hips while keeping the chest lifted and elbows high; exhale as you stand to full extension.
Reset your breath and posture between reps if needed; maintain controlled tempo and avoid letting the torso collapse forward or the heels rise.
After the set, carefully step forward and re‑rack the bar on the squat rack ensuring control and proper placement before fully releasing.
exercises.description: A front-loaded barbell squat variation that emphasizes the quadriceps by keeping the torso more upright and shifting load to the front of the legs; the bar is held in a front-rack or cross-arm position. It trains lower-body strength while also demanding core and upper-back stability.

exercises.name: Barbell one leg hip thrust
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench and load a barbell with an appropriate weight; optionally use a pad on the bar for comfort.
instructions: Set up a flat bench and load a barbell with an appropriate weight; optionally use a pad on the bar for comfort.
Sit on the floor with your upper back (around the shoulder blade area) against the bench edge and the barbell positioned over your hips.

Roll the barbell onto your hips while leaning back so your shoulder blades remain supported on the bench; bend both knees and plant one foot flat on the floor while extending the other leg straight or slightly bent and held off the floor.
Grip the barbell on either side of your hips to stabilize it and brace your core (tighten your abs) and keep your chin slightly tucked.

Drive through the heel of the planted foot, extend the hip of the working leg, and lift your hips upward until your hips are in line with your torso and knee (forming a straight line from shoulders to knee).
Squeeze the glutes hard at the top for 1–2 seconds while keeping the non-working leg off the floor and hips level (avoid rotating or dropping a hip).
Slowly lower your hips under control until your glutes are just above the floor (or until you feel a full stretch), then repeat for the desired reps before switching legs.
Keep movements smooth and controlled, maintain core tension to protect the spine, and use a lighter weight if you cannot keep hips level or control the descent.
exercises.description: A single-leg variation of the barbell hip thrust performed with the upper back resting on a bench; the working leg drives the hips upward against the barbell to strongly activate the glutes. The non-working leg is kept off the floor to increase unilateral load and demand on hip extensors and core stabilization.

exercises.name: Barbell Floor Calf Raise
exercises.notes: Load a barbell and position it across the upper back/trapezius, not the neck; grip the bar just outside shoulder width with a firm hold.
instructions: Load a barbell and position it across the upper back/trapezius, not the neck; grip the bar just outside shoulder width with a firm hold.
Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart and weight evenly distributed on the mid-foot; keep a slight bend in the knees (not locked).
Brace your core and maintain an upright torso; focus on keeping shoulders down and the bar stable on your traps/delts.
Slowly raise both heels by pushing through the balls of your feet until you reach full plantarflexion (stand on your toes); contract the calves at the top and hold for 1–2 seconds.
Lower your heels back to the floor in a controlled manner until the soles are flat, feeling a stretch in the calves.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, breathing out on the ascent and inhaling on the descent.
If balance is an issue, perform the movement near a wall or use a spotter; avoid bouncing or using excessive forward trunk lean to lift the weight.
exercises.description: Standing barbell calf raise performed with a barbell resting on the upper back to load the lower legs; raise the heels by plantarflexing the ankles, pause at the top, then lower with control. This exercise primarily isolates the gastrocnemius/soleus (calves) while the upper back and shoulders stabilize the load.

exercises.name: Barbell Romanian Deadlift
exercises.notes: Stand upright holding a barbell with an overhand (or mixed) grip, feet about hip-width apart and the bar resting against your thighs; maintain a neutral spine and engaged core.
instructions: Stand upright holding a barbell with an overhand (or mixed) grip, feet about hip-width apart and the bar resting against your thighs; maintain a neutral spine and engaged core.
Set a small, consistent bend in the knees (soft knees) — do not bend into a squat; this is a hinge at the hips, not a knee-dominant lift.
Take a breath in, brace your core, then push your hips back while keeping the chest up and spine neutral; allow the bar to travel down close to the front of your legs.
Lower the bar until you feel a strong but safe stretch in the hamstrings (typically to mid‑shin), keeping the bar close to the body and shoulders pulled back — avoid rounding the lower back.
Pause briefly at the bottom while maintaining tension in the hamstrings and back; keep your head in a neutral alignment (looking a few feet in front of you).
Exhale and drive your hips forward by contracting the glutes and hamstrings to return to the standing start position; do not hyperextend the lower back at the top.
Control the descent and ascent on every rep, using a weight that lets you maintain a neutral spine and full hip hinge pattern.
If grip is limiting, switch to a mixed grip or use straps; always prioritize form over load and stop if you feel lumbar rounding or sharp pain.
exercises.description: A bilateral hip-hinge performed from standing that emphasizes the hamstrings by lowering a loaded barbell along the thighs with a neutral spine and slight knee bend; returned to standing by driving the hips forward and squeezing the glutes. Builds posterior chain strength and improves hip extension.

exercises.name: Lever Seated Leg Curl
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat and backrest so your hips and knees align with the machine's pivot; select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Adjust the seat and backrest so your hips and knees align with the machine's pivot; select an appropriate weight.
Sit upright with your back fully supported against the back pad (seated position) and grip the handles for stability.
Position the lower leg pad just above your heels / at the lower calf (behind the ankles) and secure the thigh pad across your front upper thighs to prevent hip movement.
Start with your legs nearly extended (knees close to straight but not locked) and feet pointed forward.
Exhale and curl your lower legs downward/back by flexing the knees, bringing the pad toward the underside of your thighs; contract the hamstrings and glutes at the peak.
Hold the peak contraction briefly, then inhale and slowly extend your knees to return the weight to the start position with controlled tempo—do not let the stack slam or lock the knees.
Keep your hips and torso stable against the pad throughout; avoid lifting the hips or using momentum to move the weight.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions and adjust weight if you cannot maintain smooth, controlled reps.
exercises.description: Seated leg curl performed on a lever machine isolates knee flexion to target the hamstrings while the seat/back pad stabilizes the hips. The movement curls the lower leg back toward the underside of the thigh and then returns under control to the start.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Deadlift
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips and push them back while keeping a slight bend in the knees; reach down and grasp the bar with hands just outside the knees (mixed or double overhand grip).
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart, toes pointing forward or slightly out, barbell over mid-foot.
Hinge at the hips and push them back while keeping a slight bend in the knees; reach down and grasp the bar with hands just outside the knees (mixed or double overhand grip).
Set a neutral spine (no rounding), pull shoulder blades down and back, lift the chest and brace the abs tightly.
Take a deep breath, drive through the heels and mid-foot, extend the hips and knees together to lift the bar, keeping the bar close to the shins and thighs.
Continue driving the hips forward until standing tall with hips fully extended and shoulders stacked over hips (lockout); do not hyperextend the lower back.
Pause briefly at the top with glutes engaged and core braced.
To lower, hinge at the hips first by pushing them back while maintaining a neutral spine, let the bar pass the knees, then bend the knees and place the bar back on the floor under control.
Reset breathing and core tension before each repetition and keep consistent form; avoid rounding the spine and jerking the bar.
exercises.description: A heavy compound hip-hinge lift that develops the glutes and the posterior chain by lifting a loaded barbell from the floor to a standing position. The movement also requires strong spinal bracing and upper-back stability while challenging grip strength.

exercises.name: Barbell Front Squat
exercises.notes: Set the barbell in a front-rack position on a squat rack at about collarbone height; approach the bar and step under it so it rests on the anterior deltoids and across the clavicles in a cross-arm or clean grip.
instructions: Set the barbell in a front-rack position on a squat rack at about collarbone height; approach the bar and step under it so it rests on the anterior deltoids and across the clavicles in a cross-arm or clean grip.
Establish your grip (cross-arm shown in video) and lift the chest: elbows high and pointed forward to create a stable shelf for the bar; keep the upper back tight and shoulders engaged.
Unrack the bar by standing up and taking 1-2 steps back; position feet shoulder-width apart with toes turned slightly outward and weight distributed on the midfoot/heel.
Brace your core and inhale deeply to create intra-abdominal pressure; keep the chest up and eyes forward throughout the movement.
Initiate the squat by bending at the hips and knees simultaneously, sitting back slightly but maintaining an upright torso so the bar stays balanced over the mid-foot.

Descend until the thighs are at least parallel to the floor (or to a comfortable depth while maintaining an upright torso and neutral spine). Ensure knees track in line with the toes and do not cave inward.
Drive the movement upward by pressing through the heels and midfoot, extending the knees and hips while keeping the chest lifted and elbows high; exhale as you stand to full extension.
Reset your breath and posture between reps if needed; maintain controlled tempo and avoid letting the torso collapse forward or the heels rise.
After the set, carefully step forward and re‑rack the bar on the squat rack ensuring control and proper placement before fully releasing.
exercises.description: A front-loaded barbell squat variation that emphasizes the quadriceps by keeping the torso more upright and shifting load to the front of the legs; the bar is held in a front-rack or cross-arm position. It trains lower-body strength while also demanding core and upper-back stability.

exercises.name: Lever Leg Extension
exercises.notes: Adjust the machine so the knee joint lines up with the lever pivot and place the padded roller just above the ankles; select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Adjust the machine so the knee joint lines up with the lever pivot and place the padded roller just above the ankles; select an appropriate weight.
Sit upright with your back flat against the backrest, feet under the roller pad and hands holding the side handles or seat edges for stability.
Start with knees bent at about 90 degrees (or slightly less) and toes pointing forward; brace your core to keep the torso still.
Exhale and extend your knees by contracting the quads, pushing the roller pad upward until your legs are nearly straight — do not lock the knees.
Pause briefly at the top to feel the contraction in the quads.
Inhale and slowly lower the weight with control back to the starting knee angle, keeping tension in the quads throughout.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions. Avoid using momentum, excessive trunk lean, or hyperextending the knees.
exercises.description: Seated leg extension on a lever/machine that isolates the quadriceps by extending the knee against a padded roller. The movement is performed from a seated, back-supported position and focuses on controlled knee extension and return.

exercises.name: Lever Lying Leg Curl
exercises.notes: Adjust the machine so the hip support pad allows your hips to stay flat on the bench and the lever pivot aligns with your knees; select appropriate weight.
instructions: Adjust the machine so the hip support pad allows your hips to stay flat on the bench and the lever pivot aligns with your knees; select appropriate weight.
Lie prone (face down) on the bench with your knees just off the edge, chest supported, and hook your lower calves/ankles under the padded lever.
Grip the handles or edge of the bench for stability and keep your hips pressed into the bench throughout the movement.
Exhale and curl your heels up toward your glutes by flexing the knees until you reach a full but comfortable contraction.
Pause briefly at the top and squeeze the hamstrings and glutes.
Inhale and slowly lower the lever back to the start with controlled extension of the knees, avoiding letting the hips rise.
Perform repetitions with steady tempo, focusing on full range of motion and control rather than momentum.
exercises.description: Prone (face down) leg curl performed on a lever machine that isolates the hamstrings by bending the knees to lift a pad toward the glutes. The movement emphasizes knee flexion with a controlled eccentric return.

exercises.name: Lever Seated Leg Curl
exercises.notes: Sit upright on the seated leg curl machine with your back firmly against the backrest and feet positioned under the lower padded roller just above the heels/ankles.
instructions: Sit upright on the seated leg curl machine with your back firmly against the backrest and feet positioned under the lower padded roller just above the heels/ankles.
Adjust the thigh/pelvic pad so it rests snugly on top of your thighs to prevent your hips from rising during the movement; grip the handles for stability.
Start with knees nearly extended but not fully locked, torso upright and core engaged to stabilize the spine.
Exhale and curl the lower leg by flexing the knees, driving the padded roller down and back toward your glutes in a controlled motion.
Pause and squeeze the hamstrings at the top of the movement without lifting your hips off the seat.
Inhale and slowly return to the start position, fully extending the knees under control without letting the weight slam or locking the knees.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions while keeping the torso still and avoiding swinging or using momentum.
exercises.description: Seated leg curl on a lever machine isolates knee flexion to train the hamstrings, with assistance from the glutes and calves. Performed seated with a padded lever that is pulled by bending the knees and bringing the heels toward the buttocks.

exercises.name: Lever Leg Extension (Inward Toes)
exercises.notes: Sit upright on the leg extension machine with your back firmly against the back pad and adjust the seat so the machine's knee pivot lines up with your knee joint.
instructions: Sit upright on the leg extension machine with your back firmly against the back pad and adjust the seat so the machine's knee pivot lines up with your knee joint.
Position the lower pad just above your ankles and place your feet on the footpad with your toes turned inward (feet slightly internally rotated). Grip the side handles or seat for stability.
Start with knees bent at about 90 degrees (or the machine's start position). Brace your core and keep your spine neutral; do not arch your lower back.
Exhale and extend your knees, pushing the pad upward until your legs are nearly straight but avoid locking the knees at full extension.
Pause briefly at the top, maintaining tension in the quads, then inhale and lower the weight slowly and with control back to the start position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining the inward toe position and steady tempo throughout. Stop if you feel sharp knee pain and ensure the movement is smooth and controlled.
exercises.description: Seated leg extension performed on a lever/machine with the toes turned inward; the movement isolates knee extension to target the quadriceps while the inward toe position slightly alters quad activation. The torso stays seated against the back pad and the lower shins press into the machine pad as you extend and lower the legs under control.

exercises.name: Lever Seated Calf Raise (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Place the balls of your feet on the edge of the foot platform with heels hanging freely off the platform.
instructions: Sit on the seated calf raise machine so your knees are bent at about 90 degrees and your back is upright (seated position).
Place the balls of your feet on the edge of the foot platform with heels hanging freely off the platform.
Position the padded lever across your thighs just above the knees and load the desired plates on the lever (plate-loaded).
Grasp the handles or machine grips for stability and brace your core.
Begin with your heels lowered until you feel a stretch in the calves (ankle dorsiflexion).
Drive through the balls of your feet to raise your heels as high as possible (ankle plantarflexion), lifting the lever and plates.
Pause and squeeze your calves at the top for 1–2 seconds while keeping knees bent and torso stable.
Lower your heels slowly and under control back to the starting stretched position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, avoiding bouncing or using hip/knee extension to assist the movement.
exercises.description: Seated, plate-loaded calf raise that isolates the calf musculature by plantarflexing the ankles while the knees are bent; the lever pad rests on the thighs and weight is applied via a plate-loaded lever. Performed from a seated position to emphasize the soleus and overall calf development.

exercises.name: Lever Rotary Calf
exercises.notes: Sit upright on the machine with your back against the pad and knees bent at about 90 degrees; adjust the thigh pad so it rests firmly on your thighs.
instructions: Sit upright on the machine with your back against the pad and knees bent at about 90 degrees; adjust the thigh pad so it rests firmly on your thighs.
Place the balls of your feet on the footplate with heels hanging off the edge; grip the handles at your sides for stability.
Start with your ankles dorsiflexed (heels lowered) to feel a stretch in the calves.
Exhale and press down through the balls of your feet to plantarflex the ankles, raising your heels as high as comfortably possible while maintaining control.
If the machine allows rotary motion, rotate the footplate slightly inward or outward across sets or reps to emphasize medial or lateral calf fibers while maintaining the same plantarflexion pattern.
Pause briefly at the top for maximal contraction, then inhale and slowly lower your heels back to the starting stretched position with a controlled tempo.
Keep the movement smooth, avoid bouncing, and maintain a stable seated posture (do not lift hips or lock knees).
exercises.description: Seated lever rotary calf is a seated plantarflexion exercise performed on a lever/rotary calf machine that isolates the calf muscles through a full range of motion, often allowing a slight rotary/angle change at the footplate to target different calf fibers.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.
